subject: Chemistry
question_text: What is the electron configuration of a chlorine atom (atomic number 17)?
markscheme: Chlorine has 17 electrons arranged as 2, 8, 7 (2 in the first shell, 8 in the second, 7 in the third). This makes it one electron short of a full outer shell. [1]

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Define the term isotope and explain why isotopes of the same element have similar chemical properties but different physical properties.
markscheme:
Isotope: atoms of the same element with the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons (different mass numbers) [1]

Same chemical properties: same number of protons → same number of electrons → same electron configuration → same chemical behaviour [1]

Different physical properties: different numbers of neutrons → different mass → different density, melting point, etc. [1]

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Describe the formation of an ionic bond between sodium (Na) and chlorine (Cl), and explain the properties of the ionic compound formed.
markscheme:
Bond formation:
• Sodium (2,8,1) loses one electron from its outer shell to form Na+ ion with configuration 2,8 [1]
• Chlorine (2,8,7) gains one electron to form Cl- ion with configuration 2,8,8 [1]
• Electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions forms the ionic bond [1]

Properties of NaCl:
• High melting/boiling point: strong electrostatic forces between ions require much energy to break [1]
• Conducts electricity when molten or dissolved: ions are free to move and carry charge [1]
• Does not conduct when solid: ions are fixed in lattice positions [1]
• Forms a giant ionic lattice structure [1]

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Which of the following substances has a giant covalent (macromolecular) structure?
markscheme: Diamond has a giant covalent structure where each carbon atom forms four strong covalent bonds with adjacent carbon atoms in a tetrahedral arrangement, forming a very hard, high-melting-point network solid. [1]

subject: Chemistry
markscheme:
When a system at equilibrium is subjected to a change (stress), the equilibrium shifts in the direction that tends to counteract (oppose) that change. [1]
Accept equivalent statements.

subject: Chemistry
question_text:
For the following equilibrium reaction, predict and explain the effect of: (i) increasing temperature, (ii) increasing pressure, (iii) adding a catalyst.

N2(g) + 3H2(g) ⇌ 2NH3(g) ΔH = −92 kJ mol−1
markscheme:
(i) Increasing temperature:
• Equilibrium shifts to the left (endothermic direction) [1]
• Because the forward reaction is exothermic; increasing T favours the endothermic reverse reaction [1]
• Yield of NH3 decreases [1]

(ii) Increasing pressure:
• Equilibrium shifts to the right (fewer moles of gas) [1]
• Left side has 4 moles of gas; right side has 2 moles; shift to right reduces pressure [1]
• Yield of NH3 increases [1]

(iii) Catalyst:
• Does not shift the equilibrium position [1]
• Increases rate of both forward and reverse reactions equally [1]
• Equilibrium is reached more quickly but yield is unchanged [1]

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Which functional group is present in all alcohols?
markscheme: All alcohols contain the –OH (hydroxyl) functional group bonded to a carbon atom. This group is responsible for the characteristic properties of alcohols including hydrogen bonding and reaction with sodium. [1]

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Describe the mechanism of nucleophilic substitution (S<sub>N</sub>2) for the reaction of bromoethane with hydroxide ions. Include a diagram description in your answer.
markscheme:
Mechanism:
• OH- is the nucleophile (electron-rich species with a lone pair) [1]
• OH- attacks the δ+ carbon bearing the bromine from the back (opposite side to Br) [1]
• A transition state forms with OH partially bonded and Br partially broken [1]
• The carbon undergoes inversion of configuration (Walden inversion — umbrella effect) [1]
• Br- leaves as the leaving group (Br is a good leaving group due to weak C–Br bond) [1]
• Product is ethanol (CH3CH2OH) and Br- [1]

subject: Chemistry
markscheme:
The enthalpy change when one mole of a substance is completely burned in oxygen under standard conditions (298 K, 100 kPa), with all reactants and products in their standard states. [1]
Must include: one mole, complete combustion, standard conditions, standard states.

subject: Chemistry
question_text:
Calculate the enthalpy change for the reaction C(s) + 2H2(g) → CH4(g) using the following data:
ΔH°f[CO2(g)] = −393 kJ mol-1
ΔH°f[H2O(l)] = −286 kJ mol-1
ΔH°c[CH4(g)] = −890 kJ mol-1
markscheme:
Using Hess's law:
ΔH°f[CH4] = ΔH°c[C] + 2×ΔH°c[H2] − ΔH°c[CH4] [1]
= (−393) + 2(−286) − (−890) [1]
= −393 − 572 + 890
= −75 kJ mol-1 [1]

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What is the pH of a 0.01 mol dm<sup>-3</sup> solution of hydrochloric acid?
markscheme:
HCl is a strong acid and fully dissociates: [H+] = 0.01 = 10-2 mol dm-3
pH = −log[H+] = −log(10-2) = 2 [1]

subject: Chemistry
question_text: A student performs an acid-base titration. 25.0 cm<sup>3</sup> of NaOH solution is placed in a conical flask. Hydrochloric acid of concentration 0.100 mol dm<sup>-3</sup> is added from a burette. The endpoint is reached after 22.5 cm<sup>3</sup> of HCl is added. Calculate the concentration of the NaOH solution.
markscheme:
Equation: NaOH + HCl → NaCl + H2O (1:1 ratio) [1]

Moles of HCl = (22.5/1000) × 0.100 = 0.00225 mol [1]

Moles of NaOH = moles of HCl = 0.00225 mol (1:1 ratio) [1]

Concentration of NaOH = 0.00225 / (25.0/1000) = 0.00225 / 0.025 = 0.0900 mol dm-3 [1]

Award [1] for correct calculation method, [1] for correct answer with units.
